---
title: "Euclid × SPT-3G CMB lensing"
occasion: SPT Summer Collaboration Meeting
description: Status report on the Euclid TR1 × CMB-lensing cross-correlation programme — footprints, blinding, data vectors, and the road to a likelihood
draft: true
author:
    name: Cail Daley
    affiliation: on behalf of the Euclid CMBX team
date: 2026-06-29
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"

output-file: index.html

# Title-slide logos are referenced from the title-slide.html partial; declare
# them so Quarto's resource scanner copies them into _site/.../images/.
resources:
    - images/euclid_logo.png
    - images/collaborators/*.jpg

format:
    revealjs:
        width:  1920
        height: 1080
        margin: 0
        auto-stretch: false
        theme: [default, ../assets/house.scss, custom.scss]
        slide-number: true
        fig-align: center
        menu: false
        include-after-body:
            - assets/no_footer_on_titleslide.html
            - assets/figure-treatment.html
        footer: Cail Daley | Euclid × SPT-3G CMB lensing | SPT Summer Collaboration | June 2026
        history: false
        hash : true
        pdf-separate-fragments : true
        pdf-max-pages-per-slide	 : 1
        template-partials:
            - title-slide.html
        center-title-slide: false
image: images/spt26_footprint.png

html-math-method:
    method: mathjax
    url: https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/mathjax@3/es5/tex-svg-full.js
include-in-header: assets/mathjax.html
---

## A status report from the Euclid × SPT-3G CMB-lensing MoU {.vcenter}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

```{=html}
<div class="xmatrix" aria-label="upper-triangular matrix of the six two-point spectra of galaxy density, shear, and CMB lensing">
  <div class="hdr"></div>
  <div class="hdr">δ<sub>g</sub></div><div class="hdr">γ</div><div class="hdr">κ</div>
  <div class="hdr">δ<sub>g</sub></div>
  <div class="cell tpt">δ<sub>g</sub>δ<sub>g</sub></div><div class="cell tpt">δ<sub>g</sub>γ</div><div class="cell cross">δ<sub>g</sub>κ</div>
  <div class="hdr">γ</div>
  <div class="empty"></div><div class="cell tpt">γγ</div><div class="cell cross">γκ</div>
  <div class="hdr">κ</div>
  <div class="empty"></div><div class="empty"></div><div class="cell odd">κκ</div>
</div>
<div class="xmatrix-legend">
  <span><i class="sw sw-tpt"></i>standard Euclid 3×2pt</span>
  <span><i class="sw sw-cross"></i>this project<br><span class="lg-sub">(as proposed)</span></span>
</div>
```

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.orient-side}

```{=html}
<svg class="timeline-svg" viewBox="0 0 480 178" width="100%" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"
     font-family="'EB Garamond', Georgia, serif">
  <!-- axis -->
  <line x1="26" y1="96" x2="448" y2="96" stroke="#C5B89E" stroke-width="2.2"/>
  <polygon points="456,96 446,91 446,101" fill="#C5B89E"/>
  <!-- faint iteration ticks ahead (more cycles to come) -->
  <g stroke="#C5B89E" stroke-width="1.4" opacity="0.55">
    <line x1="368" y1="92" x2="368" y2="100"/>
    <line x1="398" y1="92" x2="398" y2="100"/>
  </g>

  <!-- RR2 -->
  <circle cx="50" cy="96" r="5" fill="#7A6F5C"/>
  <text x="50" y="124" text-anchor="middle" font-size="17" fill="#7A6F5C">RR2</text>

  <!-- TR1 cycle 1 — now (cinnabar marker; "today" lives below the timeline) -->
  <line x1="138" y1="68" x2="138" y2="124" stroke="#BC4538" stroke-width="1.5" opacity="0.4"/>
  <circle cx="138" cy="96" r="9" fill="#BC4538"/>
  <circle cx="138" cy="96" r="14" fill="none" stroke="#BC4538" stroke-width="1.3" opacity="0.45"/>
  <text x="138" y="56" text-anchor="middle" font-size="19" fill="#BC4538" font-weight="700">TR1 cycle 1</text>

  <!-- TR1 cycle 2 -->
  <circle cx="250" cy="96" r="5" fill="#1B1611"/>
  <text x="250" y="124" text-anchor="middle" font-size="17" fill="#1B1611">TR1 cycle 2</text>

  <!-- DR1 internal -->
  <circle cx="330" cy="96" r="5" fill="#1B1611"/>
  <text x="330" y="56" text-anchor="middle" font-size="17" fill="#1B1611">DR1 internal</text>

  <!-- Euclid cosmology products — June 2027 (the one hard date) -->
  <circle cx="430" cy="96" r="7.5" fill="#BC4538"/>
  <text x="426" y="123" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15.5" fill="#1B1611">Cosmology</text>
  <text x="426" y="140" text-anchor="middle" font-size="15.5" fill="#1B1611">products</text>
  <text x="426" y="160" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" fill="#BC4538" font-weight="700">June 2027</text>
</svg>
```

::: {.today-block}
**Today: TR1 cycle 1**

footprints · blinding · data vectors · estimator consistency · SPT vs ACT ·
systematics · likelihood & bias · simulations
:::
:::

:::
:::

::: {.notes}
Status report, not a results talk — the detection / amplitude work is blinded and
in progress, so I'm reporting where the Euclid × CMB-lensing cross-correlation
programme stands rather than headlining a number.

The frame is this MoU [point to the matrix]: the six two-point spectra of galaxy
density δ, shear γ, and CMB lensing κ — the whole 6×2pt. The cross-hatched cells are
the standard Euclid 3×2pt: clustering, shear, and galaxy–galaxy lensing — what Euclid
does on its own, δ×γ included. What we propose is the shaded cells — and this is a
CMB-only 3×2pt, Louis's framing: of the six spectra, the three that actually involve
CMB-κ are δ×κ, γ×κ, and κκ itself. δ×κ and γ×κ are the genuinely new measurement,
the two that bring in SPT.

κκ is the odd one out, not shaded either. That's SPT's lensing auto-spectrum — you
already have it — so it doesn't come from this cross-correlation measurement; we
fold it in later, at the likelihood level, when we do the joint fit. [say out loud]
Galaxy–galaxy lensing, δ×γ, is cross-hatched only, not shaded — it's a standard
Euclid 3×2pt product from the main 3×2pt group already, not something new we're
adding, so later slides that used to feature it (the data-vector slide) don't
anymore.

Why bother: the DES×SPT×Planck 6×2pt (Abbott et al. 2023) tightened Ω_m by ~30% and
S₈ by ~50% over the shear-and-clustering-only 3×2pt, and self-calibrated shear
multiplicative bias at the 5–10% level. We expect the same kind of return for
Euclid — Gatti's forecast has SPT-3G × DR1-South κγ improving the (Ω_m, σ8) figure
of merit ~14% over γγ alone.

This is one piece of a broader CMBX effort: fold our crosses together with the Euclid
3×2pt and it becomes the full 5×2pt, and with κκ in the likelihood, the 6×2pt. [say
out loud]

The MoU is jointly run — I'm the SPT contact, Margherita Lembo the Euclid contact,
with Louis Legrand and the rest of the 3×2pt+κ team.

On timing, and this matters for how we plan the paper relative to Euclid: the Euclid
cosmology products are now scheduled for June 2027 — the public release set at the
May consortium meeting, with DR1 products frozen March 2027. That's later than some
may have assumed (people had ~October in mind), and it sets the pace we're working
at. The upshot the SPT side cares about: we don't have to wait for June 2027 to
publish — a paper can move on its own schedule as long as what we publish matches
what's frozen in the SAS.
:::

## Euclid footprints and overlaps

```{=html}
<div class="r-stack footprint-stack">
  <div class="footprint-frame"><img src="images/spt26_footprint_1_euclid.png"></div>
  <div class="footprint-frame fragment"><img src="images/spt26_footprint_2_spt.png"></div>
  <div class="footprint-frame fragment"><img src="images/spt26_footprint_3_act.png"></div>
</div>
```

## Getting blinding out of the way

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="46%"}
- **Parameter-shift blind**<br>([Muir et al. 2019](https://arxiv.org/abs/1911.05929))
    - same approach as Euclid 3×2pt
- **Cosmology shift** $\Delta\theta \sim \mathcal{U}$:
    - $|\Delta\Omega_m| < 0.1$
    - $|\Delta S_8| < 0.075$
- **Hidden cosmology** $\theta_{\rm blind}$

::: {style="font-size:0.9em; margin-top:-0.6em"}
$$\hat{C}_b^{\rm blind} = \hat{C}_b^{\rm data} + \mathbf{B}_{b\ell}\big[\,C_\ell(\theta_{\rm blind}) - C_\ell(\theta_{\rm fid})\,\big]$$
:::

**All data vectors here are blinded.**

… but low-$z$ bins are not very sensitive.
:::
::: {.column width="54%"}
![](images/spt26_blinding_cosmologies.png){width="98%"}
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
A quick word on blinding, since it sets what every later slide can and can't show.

We use a standard parameter-shift blind — the Muir et al. 2019 scheme, the same family the
Euclid 3×2pt blind uses. (Not the SPT-3G D1 lensing blind, which was procedural rather than a
data-vector cosmology shift.) The recipe is the equation on the
left: pick a hidden cosmology θ_blind, compute the cross-spectra it predicts, take the
difference from the fiducial prediction, window it through the same bandpower operator the data
went through, and add that ΔCℓ to the data vector — and only the data. The theory curves stay
fiducial and the covariance is untouched, so every bandpower shape and every error-bar size you
see today is honest; only the absolute amplitude is hidden. It's additive in Cℓ, so unblinding
is just subtracting the same ΔCℓ.

The hidden cosmology is drawn from the public list on the right — a hundred cosmologies drawn
uniformly around the Planck-18 fiducial in the Ωm–S8 plane (the magnitude is in the bullet and
below), S8 being the lensing amplitude the cross is most sensitive to. You can see the cloud stretch along the S8 degeneracy. The list
itself is public and shareable; the only secret is which entry we picked. That selection is
sealed on the compute node — an OS-entropy integer hashed to an index — so neither I nor the
pipeline ever sees the realized cosmology, and all that's recorded is the commitment hash at the
bottom.

The practical consequence for today: this is a status report read entirely under the blind —
shapes, relative consistency, error-bar sizes — never the amplitude.

On the magnitude: the shift is drawn uniformly within ±0.1 in Ω_m and ±0.075 in S8 — an envelope sized
to cover the S8 tension, so a tension-sized pull can't be reasoned away as "too big to be the blind, so
it must be real." That envelope is public, only the realized draw is secret. The theory we
shift against is computed with pyccl (CCL), our eDR1like pipeline, not an external Boltzmann product.
The likelihood itself, and the move into CLOE, I come back to on the likelihood slide. Bottom line for
this audience: every data vector you see today is blinded.
:::

## SPT-3G GMV-hardened,<br>Gaussian NaMaster error bars {.data-family}

![](images/spt26_cross_spectra.png){width="100%" fig-align="center"}

::: {.notes}
This is the data-vector slide — what we measure, not how it fits.

Two rows now, not three — this is the CMB-only 3×2pt, per Louis's point: of the
six-by-two-point's six spectra, the three that actually involve CMB-κ are
shear×κ, clustering×κ, and κκ itself — and κκ folds in only at the likelihood
level, we don't measure it directly, so it doesn't get a panel either. Galaxy–
galaxy lensing, which used to be the third row here, is a standard Euclid 3×2pt
product from the main 3×2pt group already — not something new this MoU is
adding — so it's off this slide. (Still happy to show the backup 6×6
source×lens matrix, spt26_ggl_matrix.png, if anyone asks.) Dropping that row
also means these panels are bigger than before.

Both rows: the SPT-3G GMV profile-hardened estimator, six tomographic bins,
NaMaster pseudo-Cℓ bandpowers on the joint Euclid × SPT mask.

On error bars, since Louis asked directly: these are the full data-fiducial
Gaussian NaMaster covariance now, not the per-bandpower Knox diagonal this
figure used before — same treatment as the kappa-constraints slide later on.
I put that on the slide title itself rather than leave it to the notes, since
it's exactly the kind of thing this audience wants to know without having to ask.

Each panel also carries its own blinded detection S/N — root chi-squared-zero
against the no-signal null, same blind-safe definition as the estimator and
cross-survey slides. The message: two crosses, one estimator, one binning,
tracking Planck-18, with both the shape and the detection strength visible;
the amplitude itself stays off this status report.
:::

## Estimators are consistent, GMV is lowest {.data-family}

::: {.fullfig}
![](images/spt26_estimator_robustness.png)
:::

::: {.notes}
This is a status check on whether the cross depends on which SPT lensing estimator we
feed it. I'm showing one representative high-z bin (bin 5), the two CMB-lensing crosses stacked
top and bottom — cosmic shear × κ and galaxy clustering × κ; the same picture holds across all
six bins. Each panel overlays three SPT-3G estimators — GMV, the SQE pol-only reconstruction
(PP, polarization-only standard quadratic estimator), and the bias + profile-hardened GMV
(GMVbhTTprf) — and the hardened reconstruction sits right on top of GMV; the SQE pol-only
tracks them but with more scatter at high ell.

The message: swapping GMV for the bias + profile-hardened reconstruction — the product
recommended for cross-correlation — moves every bandpower by less than one sigma. Pull RMS
0.29 sigma in both gamma-kappa and delta_g-kappa, all ninety bandpowers inside one sigma, and
the detection S/N doesn't drop — if anything it goes up. The SQE pol-only reconstruction
(PP) is noisier and scatters more at high ell — that's single-channel
reconstruction noise, not a signal or foreground effect — but it stays broadly consistent
within the errors. So the measurement is estimator-robust.

Be careful here, and I want to be honest with this audience because you know lensing
biases cold: this is NOT a foreground-clean certificate. A tSZ or CIB contribution that
is common to GMV and the hardened estimator cancels in the difference — the hardening
correction being small bounds the *part that differs between estimators*, not the total
foreground bias. The direct test is a Websky or Agora-with-HOD recovery, which is
reserved and not yet run. So: estimator-robust, hardening correction bounded below one
sigma — and that's the claim, no more.
:::

## SPT and ACT are consistent, SPT is higher S/N {.data-family}

::: {.fullfig}
![](images/spt26_cross_survey.png)

::: {.fullfig-note .chi2-note}
Naive PTE, considerable<br>footprint overlap
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
This is the cross-survey check. Same Euclid tracers, crossed against two independent
CMB-lensing reconstructions — SPT-3G GMV-hardened in red, ACT DR6 in teal — both laid over the
fiducial theory curve. I'm showing one representative high-z bin (bin 5): shear×κ on top,
clustering×κ on the bottom; the same picture holds across all six bins.

The message is just: we have two κ surveys over this southern overlap, and we can put both
through the identical pipeline on the identical bins. I'm deliberately *not* making an
amplitude or consistency claim here — that arc is blinded and in progress. The transferable
point is that the cross is reproducible across reconstructions, and that gives us a lever:
where SPT constrains better we lean on SPT, where ACT does we lean on ACT.

[SECOND ANGLE — the next slide.] The direct "who constrains where" view: markers placed on
theory, only the error bars real, so the σ(ℓ) of each survey reads off directly — the exact
full Gaussian diagonal for both surveys. ACT is marginally tighter only at the largest scales
(the lowest 2–3 bandpowers, ℓ≲180); **SPT is tighter across the bulk of the range** (ℓ≳200),
by a σ ratio of ~1.5× through the mid-range and up to ~2.5× at ℓ≈2700 — its depth and
resolution win everywhere but the very largest scales.
:::

## SPT-3G tighter for ℓ > 200 {.data-family}

::: {.fullfig}
![](images/spt26_kappa_constraints.png)

::: {.fullfig-note .scale-cuts}
Time to start thinking<br>about scale cuts…
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
This is the "who constrains where" view — it answers the lever question from the previous
slide. The markers are placed *exactly on the fiducial theory curve* — there's no measured
amplitude here — and each carries its survey's real per-bandpower error bar, σ from the
on-disk covariance. So the whole figure is about the *size* of the error bar, and it's
blinding-safe by construction. Both CMB-lensing crosses: shear×κ on top, clustering×κ on the
bottom — the picture is the same in both.

The story is a crossover around ℓ≈200, and it favors SPT almost everywhere. ACT DR6 is
marginally tighter — by under 10% — only at the very largest scales, the lowest two or three
bandpowers (ℓ ≲ 180), where its wider Euclid overlap gives it more modes. From ℓ≈200 on,
SPT-3G GMV is tighter: its error bar is smaller by a factor of about 1.5 through the mid-range,
growing to about 2.5 by ℓ≈2700, where its depth and angular resolution dominate. The shaded band marks the
SPT-tighter region. So the honest takeaway for this audience: across the bulk of the usable
range SPT is the better-constraining reconstruction on these bins, with ACT competitive only
at the very largest scales.

If asked about the covariance — and this audience will ask: these are the **exact full
data-fiducial Gaussian** per-bandpower σ — the covariance diagonal — for *both* surveys, now
that the full ACT Gaussian exists on disk alongside SPT's. No Knox approximation, no bound: I
slice the bin-5 κ-cross block straight out of each survey's 24-spectrum NaMaster Gaussian
covariance. The crossover sits at ℓ≈200 (γ×κ flips by ℓ≈176, δ_g×κ by ℓ≈221), and it is
*lower* than the Knox-diagonal figure that circulated earlier (ℓ≈250) for a physical reason:
the full Gaussian inflates ACT's low-ℓ variance more than SPT's — ACT's wider Euclid overlap
carries more mode-coupling correction relative to the f_sky-scaled Knox approximation. So the
exact result is, if anything, *more* favorable to SPT than the Knox story. (The ℓ≈1350 number
from much earlier was a different comparison — combined-tomography on the older SACC vector —
not this like-for-like bin-5 one.)
:::

## Systematics: extinction is sub-σ for shear, ~1σ at high-ℓ for clustering

![](images/spt26_extinction_xell.png){width="92%" fig-align="center"}

::: {.notes}
This is a deliberately honest slide — I want to be clear about what we have and haven't done
on systematics, because it's the part of the analysis that's least advanced, and on TR1 it's
the one slide where the data made the story less comfortable, not more.

The one cross-correlation systematic we've actually quantified on this TR1 catalog is galactic
extinction. The metric is the standard DES/Chang contamination estimate: X_ℓ equals the cross
of kappa with the extinction template, times the cross of the tracer with extinction, divided
by the extinction auto — that's the coherent bias the template can inject into our signal. I'm
plotting that bias in units of the measurement error, sigma, as a function of ell, for each of
the six tomographic bins — clustering-cross-kappa on the left, shear-cross-kappa on the right.

Shear, on the right, is the clean panel: extinction is sub-sigma at every single bandpower,
aggregate four tenths to eight tenths of a sigma per bin. For shear-cross-kappa, extinction is
controlled.

Clustering, on the left, is the honest concern. At low and mid ell it's sub-sigma, but it
*climbs at high ell* — above ell of about a thousand it rises toward one sigma per bandpower,
and it grows with redshift bin: the coherent aggregate goes from about one sigma in the lowest
bin to about one-and-a-half in the highest, and the worst single bandpower (bin 6) sits right
at one sigma. So for the clustering cross, extinction is not yet under control.

Two things I want to flag honestly. First, this is *different from what we saw on RR2*, where
extinction was sub-sigma everywhere — the TR1 southern footprint and the deeper, higher-redshift
clustering bins make this worse. Second, and more interesting: the bias is largest at *high* ell,
which is the opposite of what you'd naively expect from a smooth galactic template — that should
contaminate the largest scales. So either the extinction template carries small-scale structure,
or there's extinction-dependent selection in the fainter high-z galaxy samples. High ell is also
exactly where the clustering auto wants a scale cut — so the clustering cross at high ell is the
region I'd treat with most caution. This is a real to-do, not a closed box.

One honest footnote on the error bars, since this audience will ask: the sigma here is the
per-bandpower Knox diagonal — the cross-spectrum's own analytic error — not the full Gaussian
covariance I showed on the survey-comparison slide. That keeps it apples-to-apples with the RR2
number I'm contrasting against, but recomputing X-over-sigma against the full Gaussian is the
refinement on my list; the qualitative picture — controlled for shear, a high-ell concern for
clustering — is what I'd stand behind today.

And the transferable message is the title's other half: this is one template. Stellar density,
exposure depth, zodiacal light, the north-south asymmetry — those null tests have NOT been redone
on this TR1 catalog, so I'm not quoting them. One template quantified does not close systematics —
this is the most open work-package in the analysis, and on TR1 it's a genuine flag for the
clustering cross, not a reassurance.
:::

## Likelihood — what's built, and the plan

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="52%"}
**In place** — internal `eDR1like` (CCL + Cobaya)

- **Joint Gaussian likelihood** over the cross vector ($\delta_g\times\kappa$, $\gamma\times\kappa$, $\delta_g\delta_g$, $\delta_g\times\gamma$); covariance from theory now, mocks next.
- **Galaxy bias — fit on RR2 and TR1** — linear $b_i$ per tomographic bin (global or binwise), with magnification bias varied; optional nonlinear $b_2$ / higher-order.
- **Photo-$z$ marginalization** — per-bin shift $\Delta z_i$ and width-stretch $w_{z,i}$.
- **Theory** — CCL angular spectra (Limber), HMCode/HaloFit nonlinear power.
:::
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Next to wire in**

- **Shear systematics** — multiplicative $m_i$ + intrinsic alignments (IA template fixed today, not yet sampled).
- **Mock-based covariance** (+ Hartlap/Sellentin).
- **Break the $A$–$b$ degeneracy** — $\delta_g\delta_g$ tightens $b_i$ ${\sim}2.5\times$, but $A$ and $b$ stay degenerate.
:::
:::

**→ [CLOE](https://euclid-cloe.readthedocs.io) integration (Euclid's framework): theory done, likelihood in progress.**

::: {.notes}
This is the most forward-looking slide, but it's no longer just a plan — the likelihood
machinery is largely in place, so I'll report what's wired and what's still to come.

What's in place, on the left — this is our internal eDR1like likelihood, built on CCL for theory
and Cobaya for sampling. It's a joint Gaussian likelihood over the cross data vector: the two
CMB-lensing crosses, the galaxy clustering auto, and galaxy–galaxy lensing. Covariance is
theory-based today; the simulations slide that follows is partly in service of a mock-based
covariance.

Galaxy bias has already been fit on both RR2 and TR1 — a linear bias per tomographic bin, global or
binwise, also varying the magnification bias; nonlinear and higher-order bias terms are available. Note
that fitting the bias is not the same as breaking the amplitude–bias degeneracy on the right — that's
still open. Photo-z is marginalized per bin with both a mean
shift Δz_i and a width-stretch parameter — that's the "official dndz handling" we just merged in.
Theory is CCL angular spectra under the Limber approximation with HMCode/HaloFit nonlinear power.

On the right, what's still to wire in, and I want to be honest that these are NOT yet in the
sampled model. Shear systematics: there's no multiplicative shear-bias m_i being sampled yet, and
intrinsic alignments are a fixed template right now, not a marginalized nuisance — both are on the
list. A mock-based covariance with the Hartlap or Sellentin correction. And the structural one:
adding delta-g delta-g tightens each per-bin bias b_i by about a factor of two and a half, but it
does NOT break the amplitude–bias degeneracy — A and b enter as a product, so they stay degenerate.
The open design question, which I'd welcome thoughts on, is what additional information breaks A
from b — shear-ratio geometry, or pushing the delta-g delta-g scale range. No amplitude numbers
here — that arc is blinded and dropped from this talk.

And the bottom line: this internal eDR1like likelihood — a full pyccl theory pipeline plus a
Cobaya-interfaced Gaussian likelihood — is the working tool today, and we're now integrating the CMBX
cross-correlation observable into CLOE, the Euclid consortium inference framework, so it slots into
the official pipeline. The theory side is already done in CLOE; we're working on the likelihood part
now. The container already carries the CLOE dependencies.
:::

## Simulations: mocks for covariance, foregrounds, and noise

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Covariance**

- **DEMNUni / FLASK** mocks → a mock-based covariance.

**Foregrounds**

- **Agora** sky simulation.
- **Marco Gatti** building Euclid-like products from Agora.
- A **systematics-injected GLASS box** within Euclid — *(same injection could go into the Agora Euclid mocks)*.
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Noise realizations**

- **D1 mock lensing reconstruction** ($\hat\kappa - \kappa_{\rm true}$) as noise realizations for our mocks.

**Ambitious**

- Run the **D1 pipeline on Planck FFP10** lensing sims → properly estimate the **cross-covariance** between **Planck, ACT, and SPT**.
:::
:::

::: {.notes}
This is the simulations leg — where the mock program stands, organized by what each set of
mocks is *for*.

Covariance: we have DEMNUni and FLASK mocks, which give us a route to a mock-based covariance
to replace the theory covariance the likelihood uses now.

Foregrounds: the Agora sky simulation is the workhorse, and Marco Gatti is building
Euclid-like products from Agora that we can run through our pipeline. Separately there's a
systematics-injected GLASS box within Euclid — and the same systematics-injection idea could
be applied to the Agora Euclid mocks.

Noise: the plan is to use the D1 mock lensing reconstruction — reconstructed kappa minus true
kappa — as noise realizations for our mocks, so the reconstruction noise is realistic rather
than analytic.

And the ambitious one, which I'll flag as genuinely ambitious: I've been thinking about
running the D1 lensing pipeline on the Planck FFP10 lensing simulations, so we can properly
estimate the *cross*-covariance between Planck, ACT, and SPT — the off-diagonal blocks you'd
need to combine reconstructions rather than pick one.
:::

## Summary & next steps

**Where we are** — Euclid TR1 × SPT-3G, a status report:

- Maps & fields defined; a standalone self-blind in place.
- All three cross data vectors measured: $\delta_g\times\gamma$, $\delta_g\times\kappa$, $\gamma\times\kappa$.
- Estimator- and cross-survey-consistent (SPT-3G GMV vs profile-hardened; SPT vs ACT).

::: {.fragment}
::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Systematics — checked so far**

- Galaxy & magnification bias (RR2 + TR1)
- Galactic extinction ($X_\ell$, TR1)
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Systematics — want to check**

- Shear $m_i$, intrinsic alignments
- Broader budget: stars, depth, zodiacal, N/S
:::
:::
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Next** — joint $\{A, b_i\}$ likelihood · mock-based covariance · DR1.
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Thank you for listening** — and to the SPT and Euclid members who made this work possible.
:::

::: {.notes}
Quick wrap. Where we are: the maps and field definitions are built and a self-blind is in place, so the analysis is running on a fixed, blinded footprint. We have all three cross data vectors in hand — galaxy–galaxy lensing, density–kappa, and shear–kappa. And the crosses are consistent two ways: across the SPT lensing estimator — GMV versus profile-hardened — and across surveys, SPT versus ACT. That's the status: the measurement infrastructure is in place and internally consistent.

On systematics, since this comes up every time: what we've actually checked so far is galaxy bias and magnification bias — fit on both RR2 and TR1 — and galactic extinction, quantified end-to-end with the X_ℓ metric. What we still want to check: the shear multiplicative bias and intrinsic alignments aren't sampled yet, and the broader budget — stellar density, exposure depth, zodiacal light, the north-south asymmetry — hasn't been redone on this catalog. One template is not a systematics budget, so I want that gap visible, not implied.

What's next, and this is where the real work is: build the joint per-bin {A, b_i} likelihood; move to a mock-based covariance; and then DR1. (The gamma-gamma shear auto-spectrum, which was flagged as a broken spin-2 product earlier in the analysis, is now restored — the failure was a stale purified MCM-workspace cache, not an estimator problem, fixed and regression-guarded — so it's no longer a gap; it gives us the shear-calibration lever for the joint fit.)

Deliberately no amplitude number on this slide — that arc is blinded and in progress, so I'm reporting status, not a result.

Thanks to the Euclid CMBX SWG — Margherita Lembo, Louis Legrand, Giulio Fabbian and the 3×2pt+κ team — and to the SPT-3G lensing team, whose winter GMV kappa maps make this whole cross possible. And — chains soon: the joint likelihood is next up. Happy to take questions.
:::
